name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ لَّقَدْ كَانَ فِى يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (٧)
```

```english
In Joseph and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers. (7)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَبِينَا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبَانَا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٨)
```

```english
When they said, ‘Surely Joseph and his brother are dearer to our father than [the rest of] us, though we are a hardy band. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’ (8)
```

```arabic
ٱقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ أَوِ ٱطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضًا يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ قَوْمًا صَـٰلِحِينَ (٩)
```

```english
‘Kill Joseph or cast him away into some [distant] land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively towards you, and thereafter you may become a righteous lot.’ (9)
```

```arabic
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ ٱلسَّيَّارَةِ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٠)
```

```english
One of them said, ‘Do not kill Joseph, but cast him into the recess of a well so that some caravan may pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’ (10)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَا مَا لَكَ لَا تَأْمَ۫نَّا عَلَىٰ يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَنَـٰصِحُونَ (١١)
```

```english
They said, ‘Father! Why is it that you do not trust us with Joseph? We are indeed his well-wishers. (11)
```

```arabic
أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنَا غَدًا يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَـٰفِظُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
Let him go with us tomorrow so that he may eat lots of fruits and play, and we will indeed take [good] care of him.’ (12)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنِّى لَيَحْزُنُنِىٓ أَن تَذْهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَخَافُ أَن يَأْكُلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَأَنتُمْ عَنْهُ غَـٰفِلُونَ (١٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘It really upsets me that you should take him away, and I fear the wolf may eat him while you are oblivious of him.’ (13)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a hardy band, then we will indeed be losers!’ (14)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَأَجْمَعُوٓا۟ أَن يَجْعَلُوهُ فِى غَيَـٰبَتِ ٱلْجُبِّ ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُم بِأَمْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
So when they took him away and conspired to put him into the recess of a well, We revealed to him, ‘[A day will come when] you will surely inform them about this affair of theirs while they are not aware [of your identity].’ (15)
```

```arabic
وَجَآءُوٓ أَبَاهُمْ عِشَآءً يَبْكُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
In the evening, they came weeping to their father. (16)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَبَانَآ إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنَا يُوسُفَ عِندَ مَتَـٰعِنَا فَأَكَلَهُ ٱلذِّئْبُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَّنَا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘Father! We had gone racing and left Joseph with our things, whereat the wolf ate him. But you will not believe us even if we spoke truly.’ (17)
```

```arabic
وَجَآءُو عَلَىٰ قَمِيصِهِۦ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ ۚ قَالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا ۖ فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
And they produced sham blood on his shirt. He said, ‘No, your souls have made a matter seem decorous to you. Yet patience is graceful, and Allah is my resort against what you allege.’ (18)
```

```arabic
وَجَآءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا۟ وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰبُشْرَىٰ هَـٰذَا غُلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَـٰعَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
There came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer, who let down his bucket. ‘Good news!’ he said. ‘This is a young boy!’ So they hid him as [a piece of] merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing. (19)
```

```arabic
وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍۭ بَخْسٍ دَرَٰهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلزَّٰهِدِينَ (٢٠)
```

```english
And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him. (20)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِٱمْرَأَتِهِۦٓ أَكْرِمِى مَثْوَىٰهُ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَنفَعَنَآ أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُۥ وَلَدًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُۥ مِن تَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحَادِيثِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
The man from Egypt who had bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honourable place [in the household]. Maybe he will be useful to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’ Thus We established Joseph in the land and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has [full] command of His affairs, but most people do not know. (21)
```

## (Commentary)

The narrative commences after the mention of the glad tidings, which served as a preamble alluding to the overall objective toward which the story concludes. These verses comprise the first chapter of the story’s episodes, detailing Yusuf’s separation from Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) and his departure from his father’s house until his settlement in the house of the *‘Aziz* in Egypt. During these events, his brothers cast him into the well, a caravan extracted him from it, and his brothers sold him to the caravan, who then carried him to Egypt and sold him to the *‘Aziz*, where he remained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ*» — **In Yusuf and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers.** (12:7). This is the commencement of the story, containing a reminder that the narrative encompasses divine signs (آيات) indicating the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of Allah, the Glorified, and that He is the Guardian (الولي) — *the protecting friend and master* — who manages the affairs of His sincere servants until He raises them to the throne of might and establishes them upon the seat of perfection. For He, the Exalted, is the Master of His affair; He drives causes to wherever He wills, not to where others will, and He derives from them what He intends, not what appears manifest from them.

Thus, these brothers of Yusuf (peace be upon him) envied their brother, plotted against him, and cast him into the depths of a well, then sold him to the caravan as a slave, intending thereby to drive him toward destruction. Yet Allah gave him life through the very cause from which destruction appeared manifest. They intended to humiliate him, but Allah granted him might through the very cause of humiliation. They lowered him, but Allah raised him through the very cause of lowering and reduction. They intended to divert their father’s love to themselves so that their father’s attention would be exclusively theirs, but Allah reversed the matter. They took away their father’s sight when they announced Yusuf’s death to him with his shirt stained with blood, yet Allah restored his sight to him by means of the same shirt, brought by the bearer of good news, who cast it upon his face.

Yusuf (peace be upon him) continued such that whenever anyone intended evil toward him, Allah delivered him from it and made it an occasion for the manifestation of his nobility and the beauty of his soul. Whenever he was made to travel a path or embark on a way that seemed to lead to destruction or calamity, Allah guided him through that very path to a beautiful end and a manifest, noble merit. Yusuf (peace be upon him) alludes to this when he identifies himself to his brothers, saying: «*أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ*» — **‘I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us. Indeed whoever is God-conscious and patient—Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste.’** (12:90). And he says to his father in the presence of his brothers: «*يا أَبَتِ هذا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيايَ مِنْ قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَها رَبِّي حَقًّا وَقَدْ أَحْسَنَ بِي إِذْ أَخْرَجَنِي مِنَ السِّجْنِ وَجاءَ بِكُمْ مِنَ الْبَدْوِ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي*» — **‘Father, this is the fulfillment of my dream of old. My Lord has made it come true, and He was certainly kind to me when He released me from the prison and brought you from the desert after Satan had incited ill feeling between me and my brothers.’** (12:100). Then, seized by divine attraction (الجذبية الإلهية), he turns with the entirety of his yearning soul toward his Lord, turning away from all else, saying: «*رَبِّ قَدْ آتَيْتَنِي مِنَ الْمُلْكِ وَعَلَّمْتَنِي مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَنْتَ وَلِيِّي فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ*» — **‘My Lord! You have granted me a share in the kingdom, and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Originator of the heavens and the earth! You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter.’** (12:101).

In His saying, the Exalted, «*لِلسَّائِلِينَ*» (**for the seekers**), there is an indication that there was a group who had asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the story or about something related to it, so it was revealed in this Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*إِذْ قالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ*» — **When they said, ‘Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a [strong] group. Our father is indeed in manifest error.’** (12:8). It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘* that the "group" (العصبة) refers to a party whose members support one another (يتعصب بعضها لبعض). It applies to a group of ten to fifteen, and it is said between ten and forty. It has no singular form from its own root, like the terms *qawm* (قوم), *raht* (رهط), and *nafar* (نفر). End quote.

Regarding their saying, «*لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا*» (**Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are**), the speakers were the sons of Ya‘qub, excluding Yusuf and his brother whom they mentioned with him. Their number was ten, and they were strong men in whose hands lay the management of their father Ya‘qub’s household and the administration of his livestock and wealth, as indicated by their saying: «*وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ*» (**though we are a [strong] group**).

Their saying, «*لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ*» (**Surely Yusuf and his brother**), attributing the brother to Yusuf despite all of them being sons of Ya‘qub and brothers among themselves, suggests that Yusuf and this brother were full brothers from one mother, while they were half-brothers to the speakers from the father’s side only. Narrations mention that the name of this brother of Yusuf was "Benjamin," and the context bears witness that they were both young, not yet engaged in the affairs of Ya‘qub’s household or the management of his livestock and wealth.

Their saying, «*وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ*» (**though we are a [strong] group**), meaning ten strong men whose individual weakness is bolstered by the strength of the others, is a circumstantial clause (حال) related to the preceding sentence. It indicates their envy and resentment toward the two of them, and their anger at their father Ya‘qub for loving them more than themselves. It serves as the complete justification for their subsequent statement: «*إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ*» (**Our father is indeed in manifest error**).

Their saying, «*إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ*» (**Our father is indeed in manifest error**), is a judgment they passed upon their father of being in "error" (الضلال). By "error," they meant a deviation in natural disposition (السليقة) and a flaw in conduct (السيرة), rather than error in religion (الدين).

This is so, firstly, because it is necessitated by what they discussed among themselves: that they were a group of strong, mutually supportive brothers who managed their father’s vital affairs, secured his livelihood, and repelled every harm facing him, while Yusuf and his brother were small children incapable of handling any life affairs, being nothing but a burden (كل) upon him and upon them. If this were the case, their father’s total immersion in his love for the two of them, his complete preoccupation with them over the others, and his turning toward them while turning away from the rest, would be a crooked and unsatisfactory way. For the wisdom of life requires that a person attend to each of his means and instruments in proportion to their impact. For a person to restrict his attention to those who are a burden and provide no benefit, while turning away from those who hold the keys to his life and the reins of his livelihood, is nothing but a deviation from the straight path (صراط الاستقامة) and a distortion in management. As for error in religion, it has other causes, such as disbelief in Allah and His signs, and opposing His commands and prohibitions.

Secondly, they were believers in Allah, acknowledging the prophethood of their father Ya‘qub, as evident from their saying: «*وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ*» (**and be righteous people after him**) and their later saying: «*يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا*» — **‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins.’** (12:97), and their final words to Yusuf: «*تَاللهِ لَقَدْ آثَرَكَ اللهُ عَلَيْنا*» — **‘By Allah, Allah has certainly preferred you over us.’** (12:91), among other instances. Had they intended by their saying, «*إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ*» (**Our father is indeed in manifest error**), error in religion, they would have been disbelievers (كافرين) by that statement.

Furthermore, they loved their father, honored him, and revered him. They only did what they did to Yusuf so that their father’s love would be exclusively theirs, as they said: «*اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ*» — **‘Kill Yusuf or cast him into some land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours.’** (12:9). Thus—as this context indicates—they loved him and desired that his love be purely for them. Had it been otherwise, they would naturally have been prompted to start with their father rather than their brother, and to kill Ya‘qub, isolate him, or weaken him until the atmosphere was clear for them and the matter settled; then dealing with Yusuf would have been easier for them.

They eventually confronted their father with a similar statement when he said to them: «*إِنِّي لَأَجِدُ رِيحَ يُوسُفَ لَوْ لا أَنْ تُفَنِّدُونِ قالُوا تَاللهِ إِنَّكَ لَفِي ضَلالِكَ الْقَدِيمِ*» — **‘I surely perceive the scent of Yusuf, unless you think I am doting.’ They said, ‘By Allah, you are still in your old error.’** (12:94-95). It is well known that the intended meaning was not error in religion, but rather an excess in his love for Yusuf and an exaggeration in his matter beyond what was appropriate.

It appears from the verse and the related verses that Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) lived in the desert (البدو) and had twelve sons from different mothers. Ten of them were adults, a strong and powerful group (عصبة) upon whom the wheel of his life turned, and through whose hands he managed his wealth and livestock. Two of them were young, full brothers from one mother, under their father’s care: Yusuf and his brother. Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) was devoted to them, loving them with an intense love because of the signs of perfection and godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure* — that he discerned in their countenances, not out of some personal whim (هوى نفساني). How could it be otherwise? He was one of the sincere servants (المخلصين) of Allah, praised by His saying, the Exalted: «*إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ*» — **‘Indeed We purified them with a quality of exclusive purity: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode.’** (38:46), as previously indicated.

This love and preference stirred the envy of the other brothers toward them and ignited the fire of resentment against them. Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) discerned this and increased his love for them, especially for Yusuf. He feared for him from them, was not pleased with their being alone with him, and did not trust them with him. This only increased their envy and rage. He began to discern evil and plotting (المكر) in their faces, as inferred from his saying: «*فَيَكِيدُوا لَكَ كَيْداً*» (**lest they should devise a plot against you**), until Yusuf saw the dream and recounted it to his father. This increased his father’s apprehension for him, intensified his love, and deepened his affection. He instructed him to keep his dream secret and not inform his brothers, hoping thereby to secure him from their plot, but destiny (التقدير) overcame his management (تدبيره).

The elder sons of Ya‘qub gathered and discussed what they observed of their father’s conduct and his treatment of Yusuf and his brother—how he was preoccupied with them over the others and preferred them, though they were small children who provided him no benefit, while they were a strong group, the pillars of his life and his effective hands in repelling every common calamity, securing the benefits of livelihood, and managing wealth and livestock. They felt it was not part of good conduct or a straight path to prefer these two weaklings, despite their weakness, over that strong group, despite their strength. Thus, they censured their father’s conduct and judged that he was in manifest error regarding this method of his.

They did not intend by accusing their father of error an error in religion, which would have made them disbelievers, but rather error in his social conduct, as supported by numerous textual evidences already mentioned.

From this, the deviation in various commentaries regarding the interpretation of the verse becomes apparent:

**Among them:** what some have mentioned—that their judgment of their father’s deviation from the path of justice and equality was manifest ignorance and a great mistake, perhaps caused by an earlier accusation of his excessive love for their mother. Thus, the primary trigger would be the difference in mothers due to polygamy, especially involving bondwomen (1), which misled them regarding the parental instinct of increased affection for younger and weaker children, as they were the youngest of his children.

He said: Among the benefits of the story is the necessity of parents’ care in dealing with children, raising them on love and justice, and preventing the occurrence of mutual envy and hatred among them. This includes avoiding the favoring of some over others in a way that the unfavored child considers an insult to himself and a display of favoritism toward his brother based on whim. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade this absolutely. It also includes following the path of wisdom in favoring those whom Allah the Exalted has favored with innate gifts (المواهب الفطرية) such as noble character, godwariness, knowledge, and intelligence.

Ya‘qub was not one to whom this was hidden, and he only forbade Yusuf from recounting his dream to them out of his knowledge of what was necessary. But what can a person do with his instinct, heart, and soul? Can he prevent their authority over his limbs? Not at all. End quote.

As for his saying that the origin of their envy and transgression was the difference in mothers, especially bondwomen, etc.—it must be said: while the fact that different mothers can lead to differences among children is something that cannot be reasonably denied, and its presence in this case is possible, the cause mentioned in the speech of Allah the Exalted for this is different. Had it been the sole cause, they would have done to Yusuf’s brother what they did to him and would not have been satisfied with Yusuf alone.

__________________
(1) An allusion to what is in the Torah: that Ya‘qub had twelve male children: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun from Leah, the daughter of his maternal uncle; Yusuf and Benjamin from Rachel, his other maternal uncle’s daughter; Dan and Naphtali from Rachel’s handmaid; and Gad and Asher from Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid.

As for his saying: "which misled them regarding the parental instinct of increased affection for younger and weaker children," the implication of which is that Ya‘qub’s love for Yusuf was merely an instinctive tenderness and compassion due to their youth—as is observed in fathers toward their small children as long as they are young, and when they grow, it transfers to those younger than them.

It must be said: this type of love, mixed with tenderness and compassion, is something that adults concede to the young and cease to compete with or oppose them over. You see the elder children, when they witness the parents’ increased attention to their younger and weaker siblings, and they object that this is contrary to balance and equality, if they are answered that they are small and weak and must be treated with tenderness, compassion, and help until they are fit to stand on their own in life, they fall silent, cease their objection, and are convinced by that.

If the form of Ya‘qub’s love for Yusuf and his brother were the form of tenderness, kindness, and mercy for them due to their youth—which every member of the group recognizes in himself and remembers from his father toward him during his own childhood—they would not have found fault with it nor censured their father for it. Their saying «*وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ*» (**though we are a [strong] group**) would then have been evidence against them, indicating their own error in attributing error to their father, rather than evidence for them indicating their father’s error in his increased love for the two of them.

Furthermore, they said to their father when they spoke to him regarding Yusuf’s matter: «*ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ*» (**‘What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? We are indeed his well-wishers.’**). It is well known that his honoring of Yusuf, keeping him close, monitoring him, and not trusting any of them with him, was a matter beyond love based on tenderness and mercy for him and for his youth and weakness.

As for his saying: "Ya‘qub was not one to whom this was hidden..." until the end of what he said—the meaning of which is that Ya‘qub’s whim for his son diverted him from what was obligatory in raising his children, despite his knowledge that this was contrary to justice and fairness, and that it would drive him into a trial regarding his children; then excusing him by saying that opposing the heart’s whim and the soul’s attachment is something a person cannot do.

It must be said: this is a corruption of the established rational and textual principles from which the realities of the stations of the prophets and the scholars of Allah—among the truthful (الصديقين), the witnesses (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين)—are derived. It also contradicts what the study of noble character (كرائم الأخلاق) is built upon: that a person, according to his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — has the capacity to adopt such character and to annihilate the psychological vices whose origin and foundation is following the soul’s whim and preferring the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, over every other pleasure and desire. This is something we hope for from anyone who has disciplined themselves with moral exercises among the people of godwariness and piety; so what then of the prophets, and specifically one like Ya‘qub (peace be upon him) among them?

I wonder, if it were not within a person’s capacity to oppose his soul’s whim in such matters, what would be the meaning of these numerous religious commands and prohibitions related to them? Would they be anything but sheer futility?

Moreover, what he mentioned is a disparagement of the stations of Allah’s prophets and His friends, and a lowering of their positions of servitude to the level of average people—captives of their souls’ whims, ignorant of their Lord’s station. Allah, the Glorified, has described the prophets with sayings such as: «*وَاجْتَبَيْناهُمْ وَهَدَيْناهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ*» — **‘and We chose them and guided them to a straight path.’** (6:87). He said regarding Ya‘qub and his fathers Ibrahim and Ishaq (peace be upon them): «*وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ*» — **‘and each of them We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers.’** (21:72-73). He also said of them: «*إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ*» — **‘Indeed We purified them with a quality of exclusive purity: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode.’** (38:46).

Thus, He informed that He guided them to His straight path (صراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — without qualifying that with any restriction, and that He chose them, gathered them, and purified them for Himself. They are thus the purified ones (المخلصين) — with the *fathah* on the *lam* — for Allah, the Glorified; no partner shares them with Him. They seek nothing but the truth He wills, and they do not prefer any pleasure over His pleasure, whether that other pleasure be their own or someone else’s. Allah, the Glorified, has repeatedly recounted in His speech the devil’s misleading of the children of Adam, yet He excepted the purified ones: «*لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ*» — **‘I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them.’** (38:82-83).

The truth is that Ya‘qub only loved Yusuf and his brother for the sake of Allah, the Glorified, because of the godwariness and perfection he discerned in them, and specifically in Yusuf because of what his dream indicated—that Allah would choose him, teach him the interpretation of dreams, and complete His favor upon him and upon the House of Ya‘qub. His love was not a whim at all.

**Among them:** what some have mentioned—that their meaning in saying «*إِنَّ أَبانا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ*» (**Our father is indeed in manifest error**) was error in religion. You have already seen that the context of the noble verses refutes this.

Opposing this view is the view of others: that Yusuf’s brothers were prophets, and they only attributed error to their father in his conduct and his deviation from justice and rectitude regarding them. If they are challenged regarding the sin and injustice they committed against their brother and father, they answer that it was a minor sin (معصية صغيرة) committed before their prophethood, or that it is acceptable based on the permissibility of minor sins being committed by prophets before prophethood. Sometimes it is answered that they might have been young adolescents at the time of the sin, and it is permissible for such things to occur from adolescent children. These are refuted delusions. The saying of the Exalted: «*وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَالْأَسْباطِ*» — **‘and We revealed to Ibrahim, Isma‘il, Ishaq, Ya‘qub, and the Tribes’** (4:163), which appears to indicate the prophethood of the Tribes (الأسباط), is not explicit regarding Yusuf’s brothers.

The truth is that Yusuf’s brothers were not prophets, but rather the sons of a prophet who envied Yusuf and sinned by oppressing Yusuf the Truthful (الصديق), then repented to their Lord and reformed. Ya‘qub and Yusuf (peace be upon them) sought forgiveness for them, as Allah recounted from their father: «*سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّي*» — **‘I shall soon plead with my Lord to forgive you.’** (12:98), after their saying: «*يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا خاطِئِينَ*» — **‘Father! Plead [with Allah] for the forgiveness of our sins. We have indeed been erring.’** (12:97). And from Yusuf: «*يَغْفِرُ اللهُ لَكُمْ وَهُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ*» — **‘May Allah forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.’** (12:92), after their admission to him: «*وَإِنْ كُنَّا لَخاطِئِينَ*» (**‘and we have indeed been erring’**).

**Among them:** the saying of some that Yusuf’s brothers only envied him after he recounted his dream to them, and Ya‘qub had forbidden him from recounting his dream to his brothers. The truth is that the dream only caused an increase in their envy, which had already attached to them before that, as previously explained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ*» — **‘Kill Yusuf or cast him into some land, so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours, and you may be righteous people after him.’** (12:9). This is the completion of the speech of Yusuf’s brothers. The verse contains the second chapter of their conspiracy in the meeting they held regarding Yusuf’s matter to draw up a plan that would relieve their souls of him, as mentioned by the Exalted: «*وَما كُنْتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ*» — **‘You were not with them when they conspired together and were plotting.’** (12:102).

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned the text of their conspiracy in these three verses: «*قالُوا لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ*» (**When they said, ‘Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are, though we are a [strong] group...’**) until His saying: «*إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ*» (**‘...if you are to do [anything].’**).

First, they presented the mention of their affliction regarding Yusuf and his brother, as the two had diverted Ya‘qub’s attention from them to themselves and drawn his soul toward them away from the other children. He became attached to them and disregarded the others, regardless of what they did. This was a present trial for them, threatening them with a great danger in the future, involving the loss of their status, the failure of their efforts, and their humiliation after might and weakness after strength. This was a deviation by Ya‘qub in his conduct and method.

Second, they discussed the way to escape this calamity by each of them proposing the plan he had prepared and the opinion he held. Some suggested the necessity of killing Yusuf, while others suggested casting him into a distant land from which he could not return to his father or rejoin his family. Thus, his name would be forgotten and his trace erased, so that their father’s attention would be exclusively theirs, and his love and gifts would be spread among them.

Then they agreed on something close to the second opinion, which was to cast him into the depths of a well so that some caravan would pick him up and take him to some remote, distant land. Thus, news of him would be cut off and his trace obliterated.

So His saying, the Exalted, «*اقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ*» (**Kill Yusuf**) is a narration of one of the two opinions among them regarding him. Their mention of Yusuf alone—whereas they had mentioned Yusuf and his brother together at the start of their conspiratorial talk: «*لَيُوسُفُ وَأَخُوهُ أَحَبُّ إِلى أَبِينا مِنَّا*» (**Surely Yusuf and his brother are dearer to our father than we are**)—is evidence that he was singled out for an excess of Ya‘qub’s love and the height of his care and attention, even if his brother was also favored with love and honor among them. And how could it be otherwise? Yusuf was the one who saw the dream and was given glad tidings of the most special divine favors and unseen honors. He was the elder of the two, and the danger perceived from him was closer than that from his brother. Perhaps the mention of the two brothers together alludes to Ya‘qub’s love for their mother, which naturally led to his love for them, thereby stirring the brothers’ envy, rage, and resentment toward them.

His saying, «*أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً*» (**or cast him into some land**) is a narration of their second opinion regarding him. The meaning is: make him stay or exile him in a land from which he cannot return to his father’s house, so he becomes like one killed whose trace is cut off and from whose danger one is relieved—such as casting him into a well or exiling him to a remote place and the like.

The evidence for this is the indefiniteness of "land" (أرضاً) and the word "casting" (الطرح), which is used for throwing away goods or furniture that one no longer needs and does not benefit from, out of disregard.

The attribution of the two opinions to them with the disjunctive "or" (أو) is evidence that the sum of the two opinions was acceptable to most of the brothers until one of them said: «*لا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ*» (**‘Do not kill Yusuf...’**).

His saying, «*يَخْلُ لَكُمْ وَجْهُ أَبِيكُمْ*» (**so that your father’s attention may be exclusively yours**) means: do one of the two things to him so that your father’s face becomes empty for you. This is a metaphor for his love being purely for them by the removal of the obstacle that draws love and affection to itself. It is as if they and Yusuf, when gathered with their father, Yusuf stood between him and them and diverted his face to himself; so when he is removed, their father’s face becomes clear for them, his love becomes exclusive to them, and his attention is restricted to them.

His saying, «*وَتَكُونُوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ قَوْماً صالِحِينَ*» (**and you may be righteous people after him**) means: and you may be, after Yusuf—or after his killing or exile, the result being the same—righteous people by repenting from this sin.

In this is evidence that they saw it as a sin and an offense, and they respected and sanctified the matter of religion. However, envy overcame them and their souls enticed them to commit the sin and perpetrate the injustice. They secured themselves from the punishment of the sin by suggesting a way that would allow them to commit it without the necessity of divine punishment: that they commit the sin and then repent.

This is part of ignorance, for the repentance (التوبة) — *the act of returning to Allah with remorse and a commitment to reform* — that is of this nature is not accepted at all. For one who prepares himself beforehand for sin and then repentance from it does not truly intend by his repentance to return to Allah and submit to His station; rather, he only intends to plot against his Lord in repelling the punishment and penalty He promised for violating His command or prohibition. Thus, his repentance is a tail to what he prepared himself for initially: to sin and then repent. It is, in reality, a completion of what he first intended of the type of sin—namely, the sin followed by repentance—and is not a return to his Lord with remorse for what he did. The discussion on the meaning of repentance has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: «*إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ*» — **‘Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance...’** (4:17) in the fourth volume of this book.

It is said that "righteousness" (الصلاح) in the verse refers to the rectitude of affairs in terms of the happiness of worldly life and the order of matters therein. The meaning would be: and you may be after him people whose affairs with your father are rectified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ لا تَقْتُلُوا يُوسُفَ وَأَلْقُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِّ يَلْتَقِطْهُ بَعْضُ السَّيَّارَةِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فاعِلِينَ*» — **One of them said, ‘Do not kill Yusuf, but cast him into the recess of the well; some caravan will pick him up, if you are to do [anything].’** (12:10). The *jubb* (الجب) is a well whose interior has not been lined with stones; if it is lined, it is called a *tawiyy* (طوي). The *ghayabah* (الغيابة), with a *fathah* on the *ghayn*, is a sunken part of the earth that hides what is in it from view; the *ghayabat al-jubb* is its bottom, which cannot be seen because of the darkness within it.

This speaker chose the second opinion mentioned in the previous verse, alluded to by the saying: «*أَوِ اطْرَحُوهُ أَرْضاً*» (**or cast him into some land**), except that he qualified it with that which would secure him from death or any other matter leading to his destruction—as if he were cast into a well and left there until he died of hunger or the like. What he proposed as an opinion involved the exile of Yusuf from the land without causing his destruction through killing, death, or a similar harm, which would be the destruction of a kinsman. This was to be done by casting him into one of the wells on the path of travelers so that they would come upon him when drawing water, take him, and travel with him to distant lands, obliterating his trace and cutting off news of him. The context bears witness that they accepted this opinion, as no rejection from them is mentioned regarding it, and their action followed it as mentioned in the subsequent verses.

Commentators have differed regarding the name of this speaker, after being certain that he was one of his brothers because of His saying, the Exalted: «*قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ*» (**One of them said**). It is said he was Reuben, the son of Yusuf’s maternal aunt; it is said he was Judah, who was the eldest and most sensible of them; and it is said he was Levi. We are not concerned with investigating this after the Quran remained silent on identifying him by name, as no significant benefit follows from it.

Some have mentioned that the definition of "the well" (الجب) with the definite article indicates it was a well known among them. This is good if the article is not for the genus. They also differed as to where this well was; there are various opinions, none of which yield much benefit.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالُوا يا أَبانا ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ*» — **They said, ‘Father! What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? We are indeed his well-wishers.’** (12:11). The origin of «*لا تَأْمَنَّا*» is *la ta’mannuna*, then it was elided through major assimilation (الإدغام الكبير).

The verse indicates that the brothers agreed on the speaker’s word: "Do not kill Yusuf, but cast him into the recess of the well," and they agreed to plot against their father to take Yusuf and do with him what they had resolved. Their father did not trust them with Yusuf and would not leave him with them. Therefore, it was necessary first to vindicate themselves before their father and clear his heart of the cloud of doubt and suspicion so they could take him and go with him. For this reason, they came to their father and addressed him saying: «*يا أَبانا*» (**‘Father!’**) — which stirs affection and mercy and prioritizes love — «*ما لَكَ لا تَأْمَنَّا عَلى يُوسُفَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ*» (**‘What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? We are indeed his well-wishers.’**), meaning: while the case is that we intend nothing but good for him and seek only what pleases and delights him.

Then they asked him for what they wanted, which was for him to send him with them to their pasture where they used to take their livestock and sheep, so he could graze and play there, while they would be his guardians. So they said: «*أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا*» (**‘Send him with us...’**).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا غَداً يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ*» — **‘Send him with us tomorrow that he may graze and play, and we will indeed take care of him.’** (12:12). *Rat‘* (الرتع) is the expansion of an animal in grazing and a human in recreation and eating fruits and the like.

Their saying «*أَرْسِلْهُ مَعَنا غَداً يَرْتَعْ وَيَلْعَبْ*» (**‘Send him with us tomorrow that he may graze and play’**) is the proposal of their request, as previously indicated.

Their saying: «*وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ*» (**‘and we will indeed take care of him’**) was emphasized with various modes of emphasis: *inna* (إن), the *lam* (اللام), and the nominal sentence, on the pattern of their saying: «*وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ*» (**‘We are indeed his well-wishers’**). This indicates that each of the two sentences contains a type of reassurance for their father’s soul. It is as if they said: "What is the matter with you that you do not trust us with Yusuf? If you fear for him from us, the group of brothers, that we might intend evil toward him, then we are his well-wishers. And if you fear for him from others besides us—that some harm might befall him or someone might intend evil toward him while we are negligent in his protection and careless in his guardianship—then we are indeed his guardians."

Thus, the speech is conducted in its natural order: they first mentioned that he is in safety from their side always, then they asked that he send him with them tomorrow morning, then they mentioned that they would be his guardians as long as he was with them. From this, it appears that their saying: «*وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَناصِحُونَ*» (**‘We are indeed his well-wishers’**) is a permanent assurance for him from their own side, and their saying: «*وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ*» (**‘and we will indeed take care of him’**) is a temporary assurance for him from others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالَ إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَنْ تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَخافُ أَنْ يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ غافِلُونَ*» — **He said, ‘It really unduly grieves me that you should take him away, and I fear lest the wolf should eat him while you are oblivious of him.’** (12:13). This is what their father mentioned in response to what they asked. He did not explicitly deny that he did not trust them with him; rather, he mentioned the psychological state that would seize him if they took him away, saying with emphasis: «*إِنِّي لَيَحْزُنُنِي أَنْ تَذْهَبُوا بِهِ*» (**‘It really unduly grieves me that you should take him away’**). He revealed the obstacle to be his own self, which is grieved by their taking him away—not their taking him away itself being the cause of his grief—out of kindness in his response to them and so as not to stir their defiance and stubbornness. This is one of the subtle points of the discourse.

He excused himself to them by saying: «*وَأَخافُ أَنْ يَأْكُلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَأَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ غافِلُونَ*» (**‘and I fear lest the wolf should eat him while you are oblivious of him’**). This is a justified excuse, for the deserts with pastures where livestock dwell and sheep graze are naturally not free of wolves or predatory beasts that frequent them and lie in wait for prey. It is possible that they might attend to some of their business and become oblivious of him, and the wolf would eat him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالُوا لَئِنْ أَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ وَنَحْنُ عُصْبَةٌ إِنَّا إِذاً لَخاسِرُونَ*» — **They said, ‘Should the wolf eat him while we are a [strong] group, we will indeed be losers.’** (12:14). They feigned ignorance to their father, as if they did not understand except that he trusted them with him but feared the wolf might eat him during a moment of their oblivion. So they responded with the response of one who denies and finds it strange. They mentioned, to reassure him, that they were a group of strong, mutually supportive men of valor and strength. They swore by Allah that if the wolf were to eat him while they were such a group, it would dictate their loss, and they would never be losers. They only swore—as indicated by the *lam* of the oath—to reassure him and remove his grief so he would not prevent them from taking him. This is common in speech. In the speech is an implicit promise from them that they would not be oblivious. Yet they did not wait even a day before they belied themselves in what they swore to him and broke what they promised him, when they said: «*يا أَبانا إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنا يُوسُفَ عِنْدَ مَتاعِنا فَأَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ*» (**‘Father! We went racing and left Yusuf with our belongings, whereat the wolf ate him.’**).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ*» — **So when they took him away and conspired to cast him into the recess of the well...** (12:15). Al-Raghib said: "I conspired (*ajma‘tu*) upon such-and-such" is mostly said regarding that which is a collective decision reached through thought, like: «*فَأَجْمِعُوا أَمْرَكُمْ وَشُرَكاءَكُمْ*» (**‘so conspire together, you and your partners’**). He said: It is said "the Muslims conspired (*ajma‘a*) upon such-and-such" when their opinions agreed upon it. End quote.

In *al-Majma‘*: "They conspired" (*ajma‘u*) means they all resolved to cast him into the recess of the well, and their motives agreed upon it. For one who is called by a single motive to a thing is not said to have "conspired" upon it; so it is as if it is derived from the gathering of motives. End quote.

The verse suggests that they convinced their father with what they said to him and satisfied him so that he would not prevent them from taking Yusuf out with them to the desert. So they carried him with them to execute what they had resolved: casting him into the recess of the well.

The apodosis (جواب) for "when" (*lamma*) is omitted to indicate the tragedy and horror of the matter. This is a common literary device in speech: you see the speaker describing a horrific matter, like a tragic killing that burns the heart and which the ear cannot bear, so he begins by explaining its causes and the conditions leading to it. He proceeds in his description until he reaches the event itself, then he falls into a deep silence, then describes the events after the killing. By this, he indicates that the description of the killing reached such a level of tragedy that the speaker cannot articulate it and the listener cannot bear to hear it.

It is as if the One describing the story—exalted is His Name—when He said: «*فَلَمَّا ذَهَبُوا بِهِ وَأَجْمَعُوا أَنْ يَجْعَلُوهُ فِي غَيابَتِ الْجُبِ*» (**So when they took him away and conspired to cast him into the recess of the well...**), He fell silent for a while and withheld the mention of what they did to him out of sorrow and regret, because the ear cannot bear to perceive what they did to this innocent, oppressed child, the prophet and son of prophets, who had committed no crime to deserve anything of what they perpetrated against him—and they were his brothers, and they knew the extent of his father’s love for him, the noble prophet Ya‘qub. May Allah destroy envy! It destroys a brother like Yusuf the Truthful at the hands of his brothers, bereaves a noble father like Ya‘qub at the hands of his sons, and beautifies such a hideous transgression in the eyes of men raised in the lap of prophethood and grown in the house of prophets.

When the purpose was achieved by the silence regarding the apodosis of *lamma*, Allah, the Glorified, proceeded with the rest of the story, saying: «*وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ*» (**and We revealed to him...**).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِ لَتُنَبِّئَنَّهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِمْ هذا وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ*» — **and We revealed to him, ‘You will surely inform them of this matter of theirs while they are not aware.’** (12:15). The pronoun refers to Yusuf. The manifest meaning of "revelation" (الوحي) is that it is the revelation of prophethood. The "matter of theirs" refers to their casting him into the recess of the well. Likewise, it is manifest that the phrase «*وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ*» (**while they are not aware**) is a circumstantial clause (حال) related to the revelation indicated by His saying: «*وَأَوْحَيْنا*» (**and We revealed**). The object of "they are not aware" is the "matter"—meaning they are not aware of the reality of this matter of theirs—or the "revelation"—meaning they are not aware of what We revealed to him.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: We revealed to Yusuf, swearing: You shall surely inform them of the reality of this matter of theirs and the interpretation of what they did to you. For they see it as an exile of your person, an obliteration of your name, an extinguishing of your light, a humiliation of you, and a lowering of your status; whereas it is, in reality, a bringing of you closer to the seat of might and the throne of the kingdom, a revival of your remembrance, a completion of your light, and a raising of your status. They are not aware of this reality, and you will surely inform them of that. This is his saying to them when he reclined upon the seat of might while they stood before him seeking mercy, saying: «*يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنا وَأَهْلَنَا الضُّرُّ وَجِئْنا بِبِضاعَةٍ مُزْجاةٍ فَأَوْفِ لَنَا الْكَيْلَ وَتَصَدَّقْ عَلَيْنا إِنَّ اللهَ يَجْزِي الْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ*» — **‘O ‘Aziz! Distress has befallen us and our family, and we have brought a paltry sum. So give us full measure and be charitable to us. Indeed Allah rewards the charitable.’** (12:88). At that point, he said: «*هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ وَأَخِيهِ إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جاهِلُونَ*» — **‘Have you realized what you did to Yusuf and his brother, when you were senseless?’** (12:89) until he said: «*أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا*» — **‘I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us.’** (12:90).

Look at the place of his saying: «*هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ*» (**‘Have you realized...’**), for it is an indication that this state you witness today is the reality of what you did to Yusuf. And his saying: «*إِذْ أَنْتُمْ جاهِلُونَ*» (**‘when you were senseless’**) corresponds in the verse we are currently discussing to His saying: «*وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ*» (**while they are not aware**).

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse:

**Among them:** that you will inform your brothers of what they did to you at a time when they do not recognize you. This is what he informed them of in Egypt while they did not recognize him, then he made himself known to them.

**Among them:** that the intended meaning of his informing them is his recompensing them for the evil of what they did, like one who threatens someone who has mistreated him, saying: "I will surely inform you and make you know."

**Among them:** the saying of some, as narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, that the intended meaning of his informing them of their matter is what occurred between him and his brothers in Egypt, where he saw them and recognized them while they were unaware of him. He took a cup and struck it, and it made a sound, so he said: "This cup informs me that you had a brother from your father whom you cast into the well and sold for a paltry price."

These views are not free of weakness. The correct view is what we have presented. This term has frequently appeared in His speech, the Exalted, in the sense of explaining the reality of an action, such as His saying, the Exalted: «*إِلَى اللهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ*» — **‘To Allah is the return of you all [together], then He will inform you of what you used to do.’** (5:105), and His saying: «*وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللهُ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ*» — **‘and soon Allah will inform them of what they used to do.’** (5:14), and His saying: «*يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا*» — **‘On the day when Allah will raise them all together, He will inform them of what they did.’** (58:6), among many other verses.

**Among them:** the saying of some that the meaning is: We revealed to him that you will inform them of what they did to you, while they are not aware of this revelation. This view is not far-fetched, but the issue lies in explaining the subtle point for qualifying the speech with this restriction, and there is no apparent need for it.

**Among them:** the saying of some that the meaning of the verse is: You will surely inform them of the elevation of your life, your might, and your kingdom by this matter of theirs, as Allah will make you prevail over them, humiliate them before you, and make your dream true, while they are not aware on that day of what Allah has given you.

The main difference between this view and the one we presented is that this view shifts the "informing" (الإنباء) from verbal informing to informing through the external state and the actual situation. There is no justification for this after He, the Glorified, recounted his saying: «*هَلْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما فَعَلْتُمْ بِيُوسُفَ*» (**‘Have you realized what you did to Yusuf...’**).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَجاؤُ أَباهُمْ عِشاءً يَبْكُونَ*» — **In the evening they came to their father, weeping.** (12:16). *‘Isha’* (العشاء) is the end of the day; it is said to be from the time of the Maghrib prayer until the darkness of night. They were weeping only to disguise the matter to their father so that he would believe them in what they said and not belie them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالُوا يا أَبانا إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ وَتَرَكْنا يُوسُفَ عِنْدَ مَتاعِنا فَأَكَلَهُ الذِّئْبُ*» — **They said, ‘Father! We went racing and left Yusuf with our belongings, whereat the wolf ate him.’** (12:17) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: The origin of *sabq* (السبق) is to precede in travel, as in: «*فالسابقات سبقاً*» (**‘and those who take the lead, leading’**). *Istibaq* (الاستباق) is to race one another (*al-tasabuq*). He said: «*إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ*» (**‘We went racing’**) and «*وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ*» (**‘They raced for the door’**). End quote. Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: *Nastabiq* means we race one another. The forms *ifta‘ala* and *tafa‘ala* share the same meaning, like *intidal* and *tanadul* (competing in archery), *irtima’* and *tarami* (throwing at one another), and others. The meaning is: we race one another in running or in archery. End quote.

The author of *al-Manar* said in his commentary: «*إِنَّا ذَهَبْنا نَسْتَبِقُ*» (**‘We went racing’**) means we went from our gathering place to the race, each of us striving to precede the other. Thus, *istibaq* is the striving to precede, which is the purpose of *musabaqah* and *tasabuq* in the reciprocal forms intended for prevailing. It may be intended for its own sake or for another purpose in preceding. From this is: «*فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ*» (**‘so vie with one another in good works’**); this is intended for the sake of preceding itself, not for prevailing. And the upcoming saying in this Surah: «*وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ*» (**‘They raced for the door’**) — Yusuf intended to exit the house fleeing, while the wife of the *‘Aziz* intended to follow him to bring him back. The reciprocal form does not convey this meaning. Al-Zamakhshari, the master of language, and those who followed him, did not notice this subtle difference. End quote.

**I say:** The example he gave of His saying, the Exalted: «*فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ*» (**‘so vie with one another in good works’**) is among the instances of prevailing. For it is religiously recommended that a person not prefer another over himself in good deeds, rewards, and acts of proximity, and that he precede those below him in acquiring blessings. Thus, *istibaq* necessarily coincides with *tasabuq*. Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: «*وَاسْتَبَقَا الْبابَ*» (**‘They raced for the door’**), for the meaning is certainly that each of them wanted to precede the other to the door—he to open it and she to prevent him from opening it—which is the meaning of *tasabuq*. Thus, the truth is that the meanings of *istibaq* and *tasabuq* are identical in their application to the case. In *al-Sihah*: "I raced him (*sabaqtuhu*) and I preceded him (*sabaqtuhu*) in a race (*sabqan*), and we raced (*istabaqna*) in running, meaning we competed (*tasabaqna*)." End quote. In *Lisan al-‘Arab*: "I raced him and preceded him, and we raced (*istabaqna*) in running, meaning we competed (*tasabaqna*)." End quote.

Perhaps the reason for the coincidence of *istabaqa* and *tasabaqa* is that the concept of "preceding" (*sabq*) is itself a relative meaning. The weight *ifta‘ala* conveys the intensification of the meaning of the verb, the subject’s deep engagement in his action, and his taking it as a quality for himself, as observed in *kasaba* and *iktasaba*, *hamala* and *ihtamala*, *sabara* and *istabara*, *qaruba* and *iqtaraba*, *khafiya* and *ikhtafa*, *jahada* and *ijtahada*, and their likes. The occurrence of this characteristic upon the meaning of "preceding," given its relativity, conveys the subject’s effort to reserve the act of preceding for himself, which only occurs with a competition (*tasabuq*) in the matter.

His saying: «*بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَنا*» (**‘a believer in us’**) means a confirmer of our word. "Faith" (*al-iman*) is made transitive with the *lam* just as it is with the *ba’*. The Exalted said: «*فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ*» — **‘So Lut believed in him.’** (29:26).

The meaning is that when they came to their father in the evening weeping, they said to their father: "Father! We, the group of brothers, went to the desert to race one another in running or archery—and perhaps it was in running, for that is more effective in distancing them from their camp and belongings—and Yusuf was with us, according to what they mentioned. We left Yusuf with our gear and belongings, and the wolf ate him. It is part of our disappointment and misery that you are not one to believe us in what we say and report, even if we were truthful in it."

Their saying: «*وَما أَنْتَ بِمُؤْمِنٍ لَنا وَلَوْ كُنَّا صادِقِينَ*» (**‘But you will not believe us even if we were truthful.’**) is the kind of speech used by one who offers an excuse when he is cut off from means and the paths of stratagem are closed to him. It indicates that his speech is not justified in the eyes of the one to whom he excuses himself, and his excuse is not heard, and he knows that; yet he is nevertheless compelled to report the truth and reveal the sincerity, even if he is not believed in it. Thus, it is a metaphor for truthfulness in speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَجاؤُ عَلى قَمِيصِهِ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ*» — **And they brought his shirt with false blood upon it.** (12:18). *Kadhib* (الكذب), with a *fathah* then a *kasrah*, is a verbal noun used as an adjective for emphasis, meaning "with blood that is a lie," whose falsehood is manifest.

In the verse is an indication that the shirt, with blood upon it—and "blood" was made indefinite to indicate the weakness and insignificance of its evidence for what they described—was in a state that revealed their lie in their speech. For one who is preyed upon and eaten by predatory beasts would not have a shirt left whole and untattered. This is the nature of a lie: a false report or a false tale is never free of inconsistency among its parts and contradiction between its sides, or evidences from external conditions and states that surround it, calling out with the truth and revealing the veil from the ugliness of its inner reality and essence, even if its outward form appears good.

## (A Discourse on the Fact that Falsehood Does Not Prosper)

It is a matter of experience that a lie does not endure in its credibility, and the liar does not wait long before he produces that which belies him or manifests that which reveals the veil from the vanity of what he reported or claimed. The reason for this is that the cosmos (الكون) proceeds according to a system in which its parts are linked to one another by relations and additions that are neither changing nor shifting. For every external event that occurs, there are corresponding necessary consequences (لوازم) and antecedents (ملزومات) that are inseparable from one another. They all have, among themselves, laws and effects that are connected to one another. If one of them were disrupted, all would be disrupted, and the soundness of one indicates the soundness of the chain. This is a universal law (قانون كلي) not subject to exception.

If, for example, a body moves from one place to another in a certain time, it is among its necessary consequences that it leaves the first place, distances itself from it, disappears from it and from everything that accompanies and connects to it, that the first place becomes empty of it, and the second becomes occupied by it, and that it traverses the interval between them, among other consequences. If one of these were disrupted—such as its being, in the supposed time, still occupying the first place—all the consequences surrounding it would be disrupted.

It is not within the power of a human being, nor any supposed cause, if they hide one of the cosmic realities with some kind of deception, to hide all the necessary consequences and antecedents linked to it, or to remove them from their actual places, or to divert them from their cosmic course. For if a veil is cast over one of them, another will appear, and if not, then a third, and so on.

From here, the final state (الدولة) belongs to the truth, even if falsehood has a temporary turn (جولة), and value belongs to sincerity, even if desire sometimes attaches to the lie. The Exalted said: «*إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ*» — **‘Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate.’** (39:3). And He said: «*إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ*» — **‘Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a prodigal and a liar.’** (40:28). And He said: «*إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ*» — **‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not prosper.’** (16:116). And He said: «*بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ*» — **‘No, they denied the truth when it came to them, so they are in a confused state.’** (50:5). That is because when they considered the truth a lie, they built upon vanity and relied upon it in their lives, so they fell into a disrupted system where some parts contradict others and one side repels the other.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*قالَ بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْراً فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ وَاللهُ الْمُسْتَعانُ عَلى ما تَصِفُونَ*» — **He said, ‘No, your souls have glosed over something for you. So [my recourse is] a worthy patience, and it is Allah whose help is to be sought against what you describe.’** (12:18). This is Ya‘qub’s response when he was suddenly confronted with the news of the death of his son and beloved Yusuf. They entered upon him without Yusuf, weeping, informing him that the wolf had eaten Yusuf, and this was his shirt stained with blood. He already knew the extent of their envy for him, and they had snatched him from his hand with insistence and persistence, and brought his shirt with false blood upon it that cried out their lie in what they said and reported.

He turned away from their saying: **إِنَّا ذَهَبْنَا نَسْتَبِقُ** "We went racing one another" etc., with his saying: **بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا** "Rather your souls have made up a matter for you." **Enticement** (التسويل) — *the act of whispering or seductive prompting* — means that the matter is not as you report; rather, your souls have prompted you toward a certain matter. He left the matter vague (أبهم) and did not specify it. Then he informed them that he would be patient in this regard without holding them to account or seeking personal vengeance against them; rather, he would suppress (يكظم) what had assaulted his soul with total restraint.

His saying: **بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا** "Rather your souls have made up a matter for you" is a rejection of what they reported regarding the affair of Joseph, and a clarification that he was aware that the loss of Joseph was not based on what they mentioned regarding the devouring by a wild beast, but rather was based on a plotted scheme and an enticement from their own souls. This speech serves as a preamble (توطئة) for what he mentioned afterward in his saying: **فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ** "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience" until the end of the verse.

His saying: **فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ** "[My recourse is] a graceful patience" is a praise of patience (الصبر). This is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect; the implied meaning is: "I shall be patient over what has befallen me, for patience is graceful." The use of the indefinite form (تنكير) for "patience" and the omission of its full description are intended to point toward the magnitude (فخامة) of its affair and the greatness of its status, or perhaps the bitterness of its taste and the difficulty of enduring it.

The phrase **فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ** "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience" was branched (فرع) from what preceded it to indicate that the circumstances surrounding him and the calamity poured upon him were such that nothing could be heard from him except that he follow the path of patience. This is because he (peace be upon him) had lost the most beloved of people to him, Joseph, and here they were mentioning to him that he had become food for a wolf, while this was his shirt stained with blood. He saw that they were lying in what they reported, and he saw that they had a hand in his disappearance and a plot in his affair. Yet, he had no way to investigate what had happened to Joseph, nor to search for what his affair had led to—where was he? and what was his state? For his only helpers in such calamities and his only supports to repel the misfortunes intended against him were his sons, and they were a strong and powerful band (عصبة). If they themselves were the causes for the descent of the calamity and the occurrence of the misfortune, then against whom could he strike? And with what could he repel them from himself? Thus, nothing was possible for him except patience.

However, patience (الصبر) — *the steadfast endurance of the soul in the face of adversity* — is not for a human to merely bear the tragedy placed upon him and to submit absolutely to whoever intends evil for him, like dead earth trodden by feet and played with by hands. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, has fashioned the human being with a disposition to repel harm from himself and has equipped him with that which allows him to confront calamities and tragedies as much as he can. There is no virtue in nullifying this divine instinct (الغريزة). Rather, patience is the steadfastness (الاستقامة) of the heart and the preservation of the psychological order by which the affair of human life remains upright and free from disorder. It is the regulation of the internal assembly against dispersal, dissolution, the forgetting of management, the confusion of thought, and the corruption of judgment. Thus, the patient are those who stand firm in calamities upon their feet, whom the assaults of misfortunes do not displace; others are those who flee at the first assault and then pay no heed to anything.

From here it is known that patience is an excellent path for resisting a calamity and breaking its intensity, yet it is not the complete cause for the restoration of well-being and the return of safety. It is like a fortress (الحصن) in which a person takes refuge to repel a marauding enemy. As for the return of the blessing of security, peace, and the freedom of life, it may require another cause that brings about success and victory. This cause, in the creed of monotheism (ملة التوحيد), is Allah, mighty is His authority. Therefore, it is incumbent upon the monotheistic human (الموحد), when a calamity strikes him and a misfortune descends upon him, to first fortify himself with patience so that his internal order of servitude (النظام العبودي) is not disrupted and the camp of his faculties and perceptions does not dissolve. Then, he must place his trust (يتوكل) in his Lord, Who is above every cause, hoping that He will repel the evil from him and direct his affair toward the ultimate goodness of his state. Allah, the Glorified, is master over His affair. Some of this discussion has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ** "And take recourse in patience and prayer" (Al-Baqarah: 45) in the first volume of this book.

For all of this, when Jacob (peace be upon him) said: **فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ** "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience," he followed it with his saying: **وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ** "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe." Thus, he completed the word of patience with the word of trust (التوكل) — *the total reliance upon Allah while delegating one's affairs to Him* — similar to what he expressed in his saying in the upcoming verses: **فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ ۖ عَسَى اللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِيَنِي بِهِمْ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** "So [my recourse is] a graceful patience. Maybe Allah will bring them all back to me. Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise" (Verse 83 of the Surah).

His saying: **وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ** "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe"—which is among the most wondrous of speech—is an exposition of his trust in his Lord. He says: "I know that you have a plot in this matter and that a wolf did not eat Joseph, but I do not rely, in uncovering your lie and obtaining Joseph, upon the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرة) which avail nothing without permission from Allah, nor do I struggle vainly among them. Rather, I shall maintain the steadfastness of my soul through patience and entrust my Lord to manifest the truth against what you describe—that Joseph has met his end and become food for a wolf."

Thus, it becomes clear that his saying: **وَاللَّهُ الْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ** "and it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe" is a supplication (دعاء) from the station of trust (التوكل). Its meaning is: "O Allah, I have placed my trust in You in this affair of mine, so be a help for me against what these sons of mine describe." The phrase is built upon the monotheism of action (توحيد الفعل), for it is phrased in the manner of exclusivity (الحصر); its meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, is the only one whose help is sought—I have no helper other than Him. For he (peace be upon him) saw that there is no true judgment except the judgment of Allah, as he will say in his future speech: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ** "Judgment belongs only to Allah. In Him I have put my trust" (Verse 67). To complete this monotheism with that which is higher than it, he did not mention himself; he did not say, "I will be patient," nor did he say, "And Allah I seek help from against what you describe." Rather, he left himself out and mentioned the name of his Lord, indicating that the matter is tied to His true judgment. This is part of the perfection of his monotheism. He was immersed in his grief, regret, and sorrow for Joseph, yet he did not love Joseph, nor was he infatuated with him, nor did he find the pain of his loss except for Allah and in Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَاءَتْ سَيَّارَةٌ فَأَرْسَلُوا وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَىٰ دَلْوَهُ ۖ قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ هَٰذَا غُلَامٌ ۚ وَأَسَرُّوهُ بِضَاعَةً ۚ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ** **And there came a caravan, and they sent their water-drawer. He let down his bucket. He said, ‘Good news! This is a boy!’ So they hid him as a merchandise, and Allah knew best what they were doing.** (Verse 19). Al-Raghib said: "The term *al-wurud* (الورود) originally means heading toward water, then it is used for other things." He also said: "I *dalawtu* (دلوت) the bucket when I sent it down, and I *adlaytuha* (أدليتها) when I brought it out." Others said the opposite. He also said: "Concealment (الإسرار) is the opposite of publicizing (الإعلان)."

His saying: **قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ هَٰذَا غُلَامٌ** "He said, ‘Good news! This is a boy!’" is mentioned without a conjunction (بالفصل) even though it is a consequence of letting down the bucket, to indicate that it was an unexpected event. For what is expected from letting down a bucket is the coming out of water, not the obtaining of a boy. Thus, it was a surprise to them, which is why he said: **قَالَ يَا بُشْرَىٰ** "He said, ‘Good news!’" The calling out to "good news" is like the calling out to "sorrow" or "woe" and their likes, to indicate its presence and the clarity of its appearance.

His saying: **وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ** "and Allah knew best what they were doing" implies a censure of their action and a clarification that it was a sin recorded against them for which they would be held accountable. It is also possible that the meaning is that this occurred with the knowledge of Allah, Who intended thereby for Joseph to reach the station He had decreed for him. For had he not come out of the pit and been taken as merchandise, he would not have entered the house of the *Aziz* in Egypt, and thus would not have been given the sovereignty and honor he was given.

The meaning of the verse is: A group of travelers passed by there, and they sent someone to seek water for them. He sent his bucket into the pit, and when he pulled it out, he surprised them by saying: "O good news, this is a boy!"—Joseph having clung to the rope and come out. So they hid him as merchandise intended for sale and trade, while Allah, the Glorified, was All-Knowing of what they were doing, holding them accountable for it; or that this was by His knowledge, and He was directing Joseph on this path so that he might settle in the abode of honor, sovereignty, and prophethood.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَرَاهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ وَكَانُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الزَّاهِدِينَ** **And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, for they set small store by him.** (Verse 20). A **cheap price** (الثمن البخس) is that which is less than the true value. **A few dirhams** (دراهم معدودة) means a small amount; the reason for this—as has been said—is that when dirhams or dinars were numerous, they would weigh them, and they would only count them when they were few. What is meant by *dirhams* is the silver currency circulating among them at that time. **Selling** (الشراء) here means the act of selling (البيع), and **disinterest** (الزهد) is the turning away from something, or it is a metaphor for being cautious (الاتقاء).

It is apparent from the context that the two plural pronouns in His saying: **وَشَرَوْهُ** "they sold him" and **وَكَانُوا** "they were" refer to the caravan (السيارة). The meaning is that the caravan members who brought him out of the pit and hid him as merchandise sold him for a cheap, deficient price, which was a few counted dirhams, and they were cautious (يتقون) lest the reality of the situation be revealed and he be snatched from their hands.

Most commentators are of the view that the two pronouns refer to Joseph's brothers. The meaning would be that they sold Joseph to the caravan after claiming he was a slave of theirs who had fallen into the well, and they only appeared there to bring him out of the pit; so they sold him to the caravan and were cautious of the situation being revealed.

Or, that the first of the two pronouns refers to the brothers and the second to the caravan. The meaning would be that the brothers sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams, and the caravan members were among those who were disinterested in him, showing disinterest and lack of desire so that his price would not rise, or they were truly disinterested in buying him because they sensed that the matter was not free of a plot and that the boy did not have the features of slaves.

The context of the verses does not support either of these two views. The plural pronouns in the previous verse refer to the caravan, and no explicit mention of the brothers occurs after that such that the pronoun of **وَشَرَوْهُ** and **وَكَانُوا**, or one of them, should return to them. Furthermore, the apparent meaning of His saying in the following verse: **وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ** "The man from Egypt who bought him said" is that he bought him from those who possessed him through this sale.

As for what has been narrated in the reports—"that Joseph's brothers appeared there and took Joseph from them claiming he was their slave who fell into the well, then sold him to them for a cheap price"—it does not override the apparent context of the verses, nor do the verses override the reports.

It has also been said: "Selling" (الشراء) in the verse means "purchasing" (الاشتراء), which is a known usage. This is similar to the two previous possibilities and is likewise dismissed by the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ لِامْرَأَتِهِ أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا** **The man from Egypt who bought him said to his wife, ‘Give him an honorable stay. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son.’** (Verse 21). The context indicates that the caravan carried Joseph with them to Egypt and offered him for sale there; so one of the people of Egypt bought him and brought him into his house.

The verses are wondrous in their mention and identification of this buyer. He is first mentioned with His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ** "The man from Egypt who bought him said," informing us that he was a man from the people of Egypt. Secondly, with His saying: **وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَى الْبَابِ** "and they sighted her lord at the door," identifying him as a master (سيد) who was turned to for needs. Thirdly, with His saying: **وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ تُرَاوِدُ فَتَاهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِ** "Some women in the city said, ‘The Aziz’s wife is soliciting her slave...’" clarifying that he was the *Aziz* (العزيز) — *the powerful and noble minister* — in Egypt, to whom the people of the city conceded honor and status. Then it pointed out that he possessed a prison, which is among the affairs of authority and leadership among people. From this, it is known that Joseph was purchased on the first day for the *Aziz* of Egypt and entered the house of honor.

In short, the man was not identified each time in the speech of the Exalted except by the amount required by the context of the story. For the first time, there was no need for more than describing him as a man from the people of Egypt whose house was there; thus, He restricted his identification to His saying: **وَقَالَ الَّذِي اشْتَرَاهُ مِن مِّصْرَ**.

In any case, the verse informs, in its brevity, that the caravan carried Joseph with them, entered Egypt, and sold him to one of its people, who brought him into his house and instructed his wife, saying: **أَكْرِمِي مَثْوَاهُ عَسَىٰ أَن يَنفَعَنَا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَدًا** "Give him an honorable stay. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son."

Prevailing custom dictates that masters and lords do not concern themselves with the affairs of their slaves unless they discern in the slave's face the signs of nobility and maturity, and witness in his features goodness and fortune. This is especially true for kings, sultans, and leaders, into whose palaces dozens and hundreds of the finest boys and girls entered at all times; they would not become infatuated with everyone they acquired, nor captivated by everyone they grew familiar with.
